M1

Introduction to Macroeconomic Policy Objectives

Policy

Name the 6 economic objectives:

• Sustainable economic growth

• Low unemployment

• Low and stable inflation

• A satisfactory balance of payments position

• Even distribution of income

• Economic stability

M2

Circular Flow of Income

Demand-Side

What is the 'Circular Flow of income'?

The circular flow of income shows the flow of goods and services, money and factors of production between firms and households. It shows how national income or gross domestic product (GDP) is calculated.

Demand-Side

What are 'Leakages' and what are the 3?

There are three leakages (sometimes known as withdrawals):

Income that is not spent on domestic output is said to leak out of the circular flow, reducing national income.

Demand-Side

What are 'Injections' and what are the 3?

Injections, on the other hand, increase national income, and increase the size of the multiplier effect in the economy.

Demand-Side

Illustrate the concept of injections and leakages on a circular flow diagram:

M3

Aggregate Demand

Demand-Side

Define 'Aggregate Demand'

The total demand for goods and services produced in an economy at a given price level and in any given time period.

Demand-Side

What is the AD formula?

Consumption + Investment + Government Spending + (X-N/Net exports) = Aggregate Demand

Demand-Side

What are the determinars for Consumption:

Real disposable income

Wealth

Consumer confidence & expectations

Interest rates

Age structure of the population

Distribution of income

Inflation

Demand-Side

Define 'Marginal/Average Propensity to Consume' + formula

The marginal propensity to consume measures the proportion of additional income allocated to consumption.

MPC = change in consumption/change in income

We would usually expect the MPC to be between 0 and 1. This is because when people earn more they will tend to spend more, but will not spend all of their increase in income – they will save some of it.

The higher the MPC, the bigger the increase in consumption will be in response to an increase in income.

The 'average' does not include the 'change in' either factors - MPX = consumption/income

Demand-Side

What are the determinars for Saving:

Real disposable income

Interest rate

Confidence and expectations

Range of financial institutions

Government policies

Age structure of the population

Demand-Side

Define ' Marginal/Average Propensity to Save'

The more people save, means less consumption.

The average propensity to save (APS) is calculated as follows:

APS= saving/income or (1-APC)

The marginal propensity to save (MPS) is calculated as follows:

MPS= change in saving/change in income or (1-MPS)

Demand-Side

Define 'Investment'

Investment is the addition to the capital stock of the economy. It is by far the most volatile component of aggregate demand – it is not unusual for it to rise by as much as 60% one year and fall 20% the next.

Demand-Side

Why is 'Investment' so volatile?

Completely depends on business confidence – if there is the expectation that the economy is about to take a turn for the worst investment will fall very quickly, as there is no point investing when high levels of demand are not anticipated in the future.

Demand-Side

What are the determinars for 'Investment'

Firms invest when they expect returns from the capital goods they buy to be greater than the cost.

Real disposable income

Expectations

Capacity utilisation

Current profit levels

Corporation tax/Subsidies

Interest rate

Demand-Side

Define 'Government Spending':

Government spending is spending by central and local government on things like education, healthcare and the police.

Transfer payments do not count such as benefit and JSA as it is essentially the process of transferring peoples' money from them to others to spend.

Demand-Side

Define 'Net exports'

Net exports can make a positive or negative contribution to aggregate demand.

Positive impact on AD.

Negative impact on AD - Aggregate demand would be lower than total domestic demand.

Demand-Side

What are the determinars for Net exports:

Real disposable income abroad

Real disposable income at home

Domestic price level

Exchange rate

Trade barriers

Non-price factors

M4

The Aggregate Demand Curve

Demand-Side

Why is the AD curve sloped downwards?

Aggregate demand is inversely related to the price level. A rise in the price level causes a contraction of aggregate demand and a fall in the price level results in an extension of aggregate demand.

This relationship is shown below:

When the price falls in an individual market, a significant factor explaining why demand increases is that the good becomes relatively cheaper than other goods, meaning individuals switch away from other goods towards the good which has experienced a fall in price.

In the case of aggregate demand, there are no relative price changes – prices in all markets are falling as you move along the AD curve.

Demand-Side

What are the 3 effects that explain why the AD curve is downward sloping:

• The wealth effect – relates to changes in households’ and firms’ real wealth when the price level changes. A fall in the price level increases the amount of goods and services that individuals can afford to buy, meaning aggregate demand extends at lower price levels.

• The interest rate effect – when prices rise individuals need to increase borrowing to afford the same goods and services. This causes the interest rate to rise, which reduces the level of consumption and investment in the economy as borrowing is more expensive.

• The international trade effect – a rise in the price level will make a country’s products less internationally competitive (assuming ceteris paribus holds – ie. prices are unchanged abroad). This causes a fall in net exports and thus a contraction of aggregate demand.

Demand-Side

The two shifts in AD Graphs:

M5

Aggregate Supply

Supply-Side

Define 'Aggregate Supply'

Aggregate Supply is the total output of goods and services that an economy can produce at different price levels in a given time period.

Supply-Side

Illustrate the 'Keynesian AS Curve'

Supply-Side

What are the 3 sections of the 'Keynesian AS Curve'?

• When output is low and unemployment is high , it is possible for firms to increase output without any increase in prices.

This is because:

When unemployment is so high, an increase in output can be achieved by employing previously unemployed workers at the going wage rate and paying the going price for raw materials and capital equipment. At this low level of output, there is no competition for factors of production: there are many unemployed resources who wish to work.

This section of the LRAS curve is known as being perfectly elastic: – aggregate supply is responsive without any change in the price.

Ultimately, this occurs because there is significant spare capacity in the economy.

• As output increases, a point is eventually reached whereby any further increases in output will be met with an increase in price .

This is because as resources become scarcer , producers have to either:

(a) Employ less efficient workers or machinery or

(b) Compete with other firms for the efficient factors of production

Both of which will cause an increase in costs and thus an increase in the price level in the economy.

• Eventually, a point is reached where all factors of production are fully and efficiently employed.

At this point, it is not possible to increase the level of output any more, irrespective of how high the price level rises.

This is because the economy is at full capacity – there is no spare capacity left to provide an increase in output.

In this section of the LRAS curve, aggregate supply is considered to be perfectly inelastic – ie. irresponsive to changes in the price level.

Supply-Side

What is the 'Vertical AS curve' or the 'Neo-Classical Curve'?

This curve is identical to the inelastic end of the Keynesian diagram. It’s endorsed by the neo-classical school of economists. This group of economists believe that the economy is always operating with its factors of production fully employed. There is no spare capacity in the economy.

(Context) - Before the great depression most economists belonged to the classical school of thought. During the great depression unemployment levels reached high levels. The classical version of the AS curve came under criticism from John Maynard Keynes, an English economist. He believed that as we had unemployment of 20%-30% it was impossible for the AS curve to be vertical. This led to the development of the Keynesian AS curve as shown below.

Supply-Side

Illustrate the 'Neo-classical AS Curve'

Supply-Side

What does the short run Neo-Classical AS Curve mean/show?

Although neo-classicals believe that the capacity of the economy is fixed you can produce more goods and services in the short run by:

Thus, the short run aggregate supply (SRAS) curve is drawn on the assumption that firms can temporarily increase output by employing more workers.

The AS curve is upward sloping because:

Supply-Side

What does a shift in AS mean? Differ between AS/SRAS

Shifts in Aggregate Supply

A shift in AS means that the total output producers are willing and able to supply changes at any given price level.

An increase in aggregate supply is represented by: a shift to the right of the AS curve.

A decrease in aggregate supply is represented by: a shift to the left of the AS curve.

There can be both short run and long run shifts in the aggregate supply curve, each of which requires different analysis.

Short Run Shifts in Aggregate Supply

The main factor which could cause SRAS to shift in the short run (and producers to supply more at any given price) is: changes in costs of production BUT not in the productive capacity of the economy.

The production capacity does not change due to short run changes in the economy.

Supply-Side

What are the factors affecting cost of production?

FactorExplanationExamples
Wage costs• An increase in minimum wage will increase costs of production for firms • As wages form the largest proportion of costs for most firms the increase might be significant • Firms will pass on the increased costs to consumers in the form of higher prices• National minimum wage
Impact of immigration (short term as immigration isn’t permanent)• Firms can hire cheap workers due to the excess supply of workers • This will lead to downward pressure on wages • As wages form the largest proportion of costs for most firms the decrease might be significant • Firms will pass on the decreased costs to consumers in the form of lower prices• Immigration from EU countries i.e. fruit pickers • Why might this not happen for high skilled workers?
Raw material costs• If raw material costs increase firms will see an increase in costs of production • These will be passed on to consumers in the form of higher prices leading to cost-push inflation• Price of oil, copper, plastic, steel, silver, zinc
Taxes and subsidies• Indirect taxes affect the costs of production • If taxes increase firms will see an increase in costs • This will be passed onto consumers in the form of higher prices leading to cost-push inflationVAT, excise duties
Labour productivity• Investment in machinery will increase output per worker and reduce cost per unit. • Firms will see an increase in efficiency • Firms will pass on lower costs to consumers • If this happens across the country price level will fall• Costs of producing computers now compared to 30 years ago
Changes in the exchange rate• Fall in the value of £ will increase price of imports • Raw materials and components become expensive to import • As demand for these goods are inelastic firms will pass on the costs to consumers • If this happens across the country the economy will experience cost push inflation
Supply-Side

What does a long run shift in Aggregate Supply mean?

Aggregate supply shifting in the long run means that:

This is known as long run economic growth and the same as the shift of the production possibility frontier.

Long run aggregate supply can therefore only increase as a result of:

Supply-Side

What factors can we consider to shift AS in the long run?

FactorQuantityQuality
LabourAn increase in the retirement age, increase in birth rate, increase in the tax free allowanceEducation and training could cause labour productivity to rise.
CapitalNet investment – purchase of extra capital goods.Advances in technology.
LandDiscovery of new oil fields.Productivity of land can be improved, eg. through fertilisers on farmland.
EnterpriseRemoval of red tape, privatisation, introduction of incentives by government, de-regulationManagement training and improved education.
Supply-Side

Illustrate the long run AS Shifts on a AS Curve

M6

Macroeconomic Equilibrium

Core Concepts

When does macroeconomic equilibrium occur?

Macroeconomic equilibrium occurs when aggregate demand and aggregate supply are equal, as shown below:

The equilibrium price level in the economy is: P1.

The equilibrium level of output in the economy is: Y1.

When aggregate demand and aggregate supply are equal, there is no reason for the economy’s output and price level to change. Total domestic output and the price level will be stable.

Core Concepts

What are effects of an increase in AD - with diagram

An increase in aggregate demand would have the following impact on the economy:

Generally, then, we might conclude that there is a trade-off involved when an economy experiences an increase in aggregate demand: output increases and unemployment falls, but at a cost of higher inflation.

Core Concepts

What 3 factors affect Aggregate demand?

FactorDiagramExplanation
The size of the marginal propensity to consumeThe greater the MPC the greater the chance that there will be a significant injection into the circular flow of income. Investment in particular is heavily reliant on businesses being confident that consumers will spend their money if firms add to their existing capacity. If the MPC is high for an economy businesses will be more likely to invest. Any increase in AD may not be inflationary as investment by firms will also increase the capital stock (shift of the LRAS curve) of the economy.
The size of the multiplier.If there is a large multiplier effect then the initial change in AD will have a much greater impact on AD than if there is a smaller multiplier effect. For example, an initial round of investment caused by increased confidence might lead to increased consumption. The extra spending might lead to additional knock on effects such as increased employment which might stimulate a further round of spending.
The original level of economic activity1) Considerable spare capacity – increase in AD will increase output and reduce unemployment without having an inflationary impact. 2) Full capacity – AD growth will be purely inflationary, with no increase in output. This is the classical interpretation of the LRAS curve.
Core Concepts

What two factors affect changes in Aggregate Supply?

FactorExplanation
Size of the increase in ASThe bigger the increase, the bigger the impact on the economy.
The level of economic activity.1) An increase in AS when the economy is at or near full capacity will raise the output of the economy and lower the price level. This fall in price level will be beneficial to the economy as it will stimulate spending. 2) An increase in AS when the economy is operating with significant spare capacity will have no impact on the economy – there is not enough demand to employ all the resources the economy already has. 3) If the growth in aggregate demand is slower than the growth is aggregate supply, there is a danger of deflation/unemployment
Core Concepts

What happens when AD rises more quickly than AS?

Inflation will occur – the economy could be said to be ‘overheating’. It is experiencing higher output, but this rise is coming at the cost of inflation and will not be sustainable unless AS increases at a more rapid rate.

Core Concepts

What happens if AD growth matches AS growth?

If increases in AS can match increases in AD, the economy can enjoy higher output without encountering inflationary pressures. We can illustrate this below:

Core Concepts

What changes in the economy could affect both the AD and AS curves at the same time?

Government spending on training will have a similar effect, increasing both AD and AS.

(2)

M7

Inflation - What it is and how is it measured?

Macro Indicators

Define 'Inflation'

Inflation is a sustained rise in the price level across the economy.

Macro Indicators

Why do we care about 'Inflation'?

High inflation could:

Macro Indicators

What is the governments target for inflation?

The government targets a low and stable level of inflation of: 2%.

Macro Indicators

Why is the inflation target not '0%'?

It gives firms the flexibility to achieve real wage flexibility despite the existence of nominal wage rigidity. If inflation is 2%, firms can freeze wages and prices at 2%.However, if inflation is zero, then a firm would have to cut nominal wages by 2% - Cutting nominal wages is much harder psychologically. If firms cannot cut adjust wages, it could lead to unemployment.

So if inflation is zero, real wages cannot easily fall in declining regions or industries, and unemployment will rise. Inflation, the argument runs, greases the wheels of the labour market and lowers unemployment.

Macro Indicators

Define 'Deflation'

Deflation is a situation in which the average level of prices is falling – this is negative inflation.

Macro Indicators

Why is 'Deflation' seemed as bad for the economy?

Deflation can signal to economic agents that the economy is in terminal decline. If consumers expect prices to continue to fall, they may delay purchasing goods, expecting to buy at a lower price in the future. Consumption would therefore fall, which is a component of AD so AD falls. This may mean that firms produce less output to meet a lower AD which could lead to negative economic growth and a rise in unemployment.

The UK was expecting deflation during the financial crisis but this did not mean its consumers had been behaving in such a way as to stop spending. Central Banks can have measures to prevent deflation lasting for too long.

Macro Indicators

Define 'Disinflation'

Disinflation is a decrease in the rate of inflation. This is when the rate of increase in the general price level of goods and services decreases.

Macro Indicators

How do we measure the 'Inflation Rate'?

Using the Consumer Price Index (CPI). It looks at the rate of change in prices for goods and services over a 12 month period. The current rate of the CPI stands at 3.2% for 12 months to March 2024.

Macro Indicators

How do you calculate Inflation Rate with Index Numbers? (Formula)

New price level – old price level / Old price level x 100

Macro Indicators

Exam Tip

Provided you are comparing the index number for one of the years between 2015 and 2017 with the base year index number of 100 in 2015 the increase in the index number is the same as the % increase over the data period you are looking at. In 2017 for example the index number was 110. This represents a 10% increase in the general price level. However, a change in the index numbers is not the same as a percentage change when a comparison is made with a year other than the base year.

Macro Indicators

What are the difficulties of measuring Inflation?

Whilst the measures aim to give a representative picture of what is happening to prices in the country, this may not be a wholly accurate picture. This is because:

To decide whether prices are rising the prices of the same goods and services should be compared. In reality this doesn’t happen – goods and services often improve in quality over time. So if the price of a product rises, this might reflect a higher charge to cover product improvements rather than the same good becoming more expensive.

It has been estimated that, if the price changes that reflect improvements in quality were to be removed, the inflation rate would be reduced by 1.1%.

Although the weights are reviewed annually, the measures do not take into account people’s ability to substitute away from products which experience a rise in price during the year.

Different individuals will spend on different things, so this doesn’t reflect overall inflation rate. For example, single people and pensioners might have different spending patterns.

M8

Inflation - Causes and Consequences of Inflation

Macro Indicators

Illustrate both causes of inflation on a Keynesian Curve

2. Demand-Pull Inflation:

Macro Indicators

What are the 'costs' to Inflation?

FactorExplanation
Shoe leather costsDuring periods of inflation, households and firms cannot afford to hold cash and money in current accounts as it will be losing value. Shoe leather costs are costs incurred as a result of reducing cash balances – people need to walk to the bank more frequently to withdraw or move money, wearing out their shoes. Even if you can now do it online, it still takes time and effort.
Administrative costsStaff time may have to be devoted to adjusting accounts, assessing raw material costs and negotiating with unions about wage rises.
Menu costsFirms have to adjust their prices more often when there is inflation, eg. restaurants on menus, and items such as capital equipment (vending machines) have to be changed.
Random redistribution of incomeMany groups of people lose out as a result of high inflation: • Individuals who have low, fixed incomes may find their income fails to keep up with price rises. • Those who rely on state benefits (if these are not linked to inflation). • Net savers, as the value of savings falls over time. • Creditors – they will effectively receive lower repayments because the value of what they are owed falls.
UncertaintyIf firms are uncertain about what their costs will be and what prices they will receive from selling their products they may be reluctant to invest. Inflation also complicates household financial planning, making it difficult for people to decide how much to save and where to place their savings.
Loss of international competitivenessWhen domestic inflation is higher than international inflation, exports become more expensive and fall – foreign consumers find domestic goods less competitive. Imports become more competitive compared to high priced UK goods so imports rise as well. This creates a large deficit on the current account of the balance of payments.
Fall in the value of moneyWhen the price level rises, each pound coin held will buy less. Whether households experience a fall in their purchasing power as a result of inflation will depend on whether their disposable income rises by more or less than the rate of inflation.
Inflationary noiseInflation has a distorting effect on market prices, as it means they do not signal the relative scarcity of products efficiently. For example, if the price of a TV rises when there is no inflation, we can conclude that it has become relatively more expensive. When inflation is present, consumers will be uncertain whether the rise in price does reflect a relative price rise or whether it is just in line with inflation.
Fiscal dragIf tax brackets are not adjusted in line with inflation then people’s incomes will be pushed into higher tax bands as a result of inflation, and will thus experience a fall in their disposable income whilst the government receives a higher tax revenue.
Inflation causing inflation.1) If households expect prices to rise in the future they may seek to buy more items now, thereby increasing AD. 2) If workers think inflation will be a problem they will ask for pay rises just to protect their current real earnings. 3) Inflationary expectations will cause firms to raise prices in order to protect their real profit levels.
Macro Indicators

What are the benefits of Inflation?

FactorExplanation
Encourages economic growthA small amount of demand-pull inflation can act as an incentive for businesses to invest, with higher AD and a steady rise in the price level encouraging firms to increase output. Demand-pull inflation is also an indication of economic growth
Real wage flexibilityAs workers will not accept a cut in their nominal wages, a moderate level of inflation gives firms the flexibility to cut real wages by raising nominal wages by less than the rate of inflation. This can create a feel-good factor without costing the firm any more – workers psychologically feel happy when their income increases, even if their purchasing power does not.
Gain to borrowersThe real value of debt goes down when there is inflation, which can make people feel wealthier. For example, if you have bought an asset, such as a house, then the value of the asset will rise with inflation whilst the debt remains the same, increasing your confidence and spending. Inflation can be particularly good at reducing the real value of debt for governments which are borrowing a lot!
Macro Indicators

Evaluate the consequences of Inflation

Whether or not inflation is harmful to an economy depends upon:

A low rate of inflation, particularly if stable, is unlikely to cause serious problems for an economy and could in fact be beneficial. Demand-pull inflation often comes with rapid periods of economic growth.

A high rate could cause serious problems, with hyperinflation potentially causing meltdown – prices could be changing so quickly than firms can’t publish prices.

Cost push inflation tends to be more harmful for an economy than demand pull inflation. This is because cost-push inflation tends to be accompanied by a fall in real GDP. Cost-push inflation also means raw material costs will increase and may hit firms hardest.

Fluctuating prices is destabilising as it creates inflationary noise and makes it difficult for households, firms and the government to plan ahead. Firms are less likely to invest if inflation is extreme, volatile and hard to predict.

Unanticipated inflation causes uncertainty and a random redistribution of income, whereas forecasted inflation will leave people more confident and measures can be taken to overcome random redistributions of income.

If inflation is higher than in another country, it can make their products uncompetitive.

Finally, it is important to recognise that inflation affects different people in different ways. For example, young people may experience less harm if the price of electronic goods rises slower than the general price level.

Everyone has a different rate of inflation depending on what they buy. More importantly, some individuals will gain from inflation (such as borrowers) whereas others will lose out (such as savers).

Macro Indicators

Illustrate how deflation could be caused by supply-side effects - beneficial?

Yes because consumers can enjoy more goods and services and country becomes more internationally competitive

Macro Indicators

Illustrate how deflation could be caused by demand-side effects - beneficial?

No – can cause a harmful deflationary spiral as lower AD will cause firms to cut back production, resulting in higher unemployment. Households are likely to reduce their spending for fear that they may experience unemployment and because they will expect prices to be even lower in the future.

M9

Unemployment - What it is and how it is measured?

International

Define 'Unemployment'

Unemployment exists when people who are of working age who are willing and able to work are without jobs.

International

Why do we care about 'Unemployment' and its position Macro Objectives?

Note – unemployment COULD be a good thing, in the short term, if it gives people time to look for a job.

International

What is the Governments target for unemployment levels?

Whilst the government has no specific numerical target for unemployment, it is clearly the case that successful economic management will result in a low rate of unemployment.

However, the government’s aim is not eliminate unemployment entirely. Instead, the government targets full employment , which is often thought to be around a 4% unemployment rate.

International

Why is 'full employment' not the same as 'zero unemployment'?

Because even during periods of high activity some people will be out of work as a result of moving between jobs.

It is also the case that reducing unemployment too low will result in inflation.

International

How do your measure unemployment?

The unemployment rate is: the percentage of the labour force which is unemployed.

This is calculated as:

number of (unemployed/labour force) × 100

International

What are the two main methods to help measure unemployment?

• ILO measure of unemployment

• Claimant Count Measure

International

Define 'ILO measure of unemployment'

An individual is classed as being unemployed if they are ‘Out of work, wants a job, has actively sought work in the last 4 weeks and is available to start work in the next 2 weeks or is out of work, has found a job and is waiting to start in the next 2 weeks.’

(Context) The government uses the Labour Force Survey (a questionnaire of 60,000 households carried out by the Office for National Statistics) to collect data on unemployment, using the International Labour Organisation (ILO) measure.

International

What group of people who we would probably consider to be unemployed would not be counted as being unemployed using this definition?

The long term unemployed (ie. Those who previously wanted a job but have not sought work in the last four weeks because they have given up looking).

International

What is the 'Claimant Court Measure'?

This is a monthly count of those claiming unemployment benefits – Job Seeker’s Allowance. Claimants must declare that they are out of work, capable of, available for and actively seeking work in the week in which their claim is made.

M10

Unemployment - Causes and Consequences

International

What are advantages of ILO Measure?

Advantages of LFS Measure

Thought to capture more of those who are unemployed. Some people might be actively seeking work but not be entitled to claim benefits – such as people whose partner is working or claiming benefits and those under 18.

Used by most OECD countries, it provides an excellent tool for making international comparisons.

Some people claiming benefits might not actively be seeking work – they may be claiming benefits on false pretences.

International

What are advantages of Claimant Count Measure?

Advantages of Claimant Count Measure

Whilst the LFS survey provides a range of information, it is significantly more expensive to collect than the claimant count. The claimant count is relatively cheap as records of people claiming benefits are already kept.

International

What are the causes of unemployment?

CauseExplanation
Frictional unemploymentShort term unemployment caused by workers moving between jobs. There are time lags between workers leaving their job and starting a new one. • Search unemployment – some unemployed people do not take the first job offered to them but instead spend some time looking around for what they consider to be a good job. • Seasonal unemployment – labour is only demanded at certain times of the year, for example ice cream sellers and fruit pickers. • Casual work – individuals are out of work in between irregular periods of employment.
Structural unemploymentUnemployment caused by the decline in demand in an industry leading to fewer jobs. Workers are often occupationally immobile – they have the wrong skills and qualifications for other jobs. They are also geographically immobile, finding it difficult to move to where new jobs are located. Workers are often occupationally immobile – they have the wrong skills and qualifications for other jobs. • Regional unemployment: workers are often geographically immobile, so are unemployed because they cannot move to where the work is. • Technological unemployment: caused by capital taking the place of labour due to automation. • International unemployment – occurs when jobs are lost because firms decide to carry out some of their work abroad (outsourcing) or because households and firms decide to buy imports rather than domestically produced products.
Cyclical unemployment or Demand deficient unemploymentUnemployment arising from a lack of aggregate demand. If the demand for goods and services falls, fewer workers are needed to produce the output, meaning some will be laid off. These unemployed will have less income and so will spend less. Producers therefore face a further cut in incomes and lay off more workers – this spirals into a negative multiplier effect and can cause mass unemployment.
Seasonal unemploymentSeasonal workers not being employed at other times of year, eg. tourist industry
Voluntary UnemploymentVoluntary unemployment – caused by people choosing not to work. A person who is made redundant from an £800 a week job may choose not to take up work if the only jobs available are paying £400. Generous benefits possibly make people want to claim benefits rather than work? The after-tax income for someone on a low income may not be that much more than they would get on benefits.
International

What are the costs of unemployment?

CostExplanation
Lost outputUnemployment involves loss potential national output and is a waste of scarce resources. Government spending on unemployment benefits – means can’t be spent elsewhere. When
Lower living standardsWorkers and families suffer lower standard of living as a result of having to live off reduced income of benefits.
Lost tax revenueIf more people were in work, income, spending and possibly profits would be higher. This would mean the government would collect more revenue from income tax. This revenue could then be spent on public services to improve people’s lives. When unemployment is high, the government will either have to cut spending, raise taxes or raise borrowing. Cutting spending could reduce social welfare. Raising taxes will reduce real disposable income. And increasing borrowing could raise the interest rate.
Government spending on unemployment benefitsGovernment may have to redirect spending from other areas to pay job seeker’s allowance. The money spent on unemployment benefit therefore has a high opportunity cost.
Pressure on other forms of government spendingUnemployed people are more likely to experience health problems, including mental health problems, or turn to crime. This could put pressure on health and police spending. Additionally, higher rates of marital break-up could put pressure on the government to provide more social housing.
Social costsUnemployed are likely to lose income as well as motivation and a sense of well-being. Children with unemployed parents can also suffer – unemployment is one of the main causes of poverty. High unemployment rates are linked to inequality, social deprivation, divorce rates, low life expectancy, crime. Having a job, gives people purpose which is good for mental and physical health. And being in work makes another job easier to get.
HysteresisThe longer people are out of work, the more difficult it will be for them to find another job. Employers may be reluctant to take on workers who have been out of work for a long time (signal of low ability and a loss of skills), and the unemployed may lose motivation to find a job having been long-term unemployed. Hysteresis is thus unemployment causing unemployment. All of this may lead to loss of skills for workers and cause structural unemployment.
Costs for other economiesUnemployment in one country is likely to reduce demand for exports in another country. A country may also experience immigration from a country with high unemployment rates.
International

What are the benefits of unemployment?

BenefitExplanation
Time for searchFor a few people, unemployment may give people time to search for a more rewarding job, which in the long term may enable them to produce more output. This includes graduates and people that may be overqualified for some jobs.
Helps firms growEasier for firms wishing to expand to recruit workers.
Can reduce inflationLower level of output puts less pressure on the price level and high unemployment may discourage workers from seeking pay rises.
International

Evaluating the consequences of unemployment

Whether or not unemployment is harmful to an economy depends upon:

The higher the rate of unemployment the more damaging it will be. A 3% unemployment rate is not considered to be a problem – there will always be a level of frictional unemployment as people move between jobs.

A 6% unemployment rate with people being out of work for 2 years would be far worse than a 10% rate with people being out of work for only 3 months. This is because costs tend to build up over time as when people are out of work for more than a year they find it difficult to get a job. The former would also be an example of structural unemployment which is harder to tackle.

Generous benefits reduce the costs of unemployment but have an opportunity cost for the economy.

Costs of unemployment will be higher when there’s cyclical and structural unemployment. This is because unemployment is widespread and can be on a significant scale. Frictional unemployment is the least serious.

Particular groups may be more susceptible to unemployment. It could be argued that youth unemployment is a big problem because long term economic growth depends on them. Young people being out of work for a long time damages future prospects and loss of skills.

International

Evaluating the effects of full employment

What is meant by full employment?

A situation when there is no demand deficient or cyclical unemployment. In other words, everyone who is willing to work at the going wage rate has a job. Full employment does not constitute 0% unemployment. This is because there will always be people moving between jobs even when the economy is booming. There is a time lag between finding jobs so a small amount of workers will unemployed for a short period of time. The government therefore has a target for full employment of 3%.

What are the benefits to the economy of reaching full employment?

Achieving full employment is a key macroeconomic objective. There are significant benefits if the majority of the labour force is working. A greater amount of output will be produced as labour (factor of production) aids the production process. By using more of the economy’s factors of production that are available (labour in this case) resources are utilised more efficiently which can aid short-run economic growth, a key macroeconomic objective. If there was significant unemployment there would be a huge loss of output that could potentially be produced (alongside other problems) hence full employment is beneficial.

What impact does reaching full employment have on the government?

With less people unemployed, the government has to give out very little in the form of unemployment benefits such as JSA. With more people working, the government can collect greater tax revenue, such as income tax. Therefore, the government’s budget position can move towards a surplus. More money can now be spent on education which can help increase productivity and the quality of labour, hence increasing full productive capacity. Therefore can also lead to long run economic growth (as well as short run mentioned above).

What are the potential negative effects of an economy reaching full employment? Under what conditions do these hold?

As an economy approaches full employment alongside utilising other factors of production fully and efficiently a supply constraint is reached. No more workers can now be employed meaning that output cannot rise to meet a potential rise in AD. As a result the economy can’t experience economic growth with the effect of the rise in AD being purely inflationary, thus harming a key macroeconomic objective (price stability).

If there are more workers who are unemployed and could potentially replace existing workers, workers would be motivated to work harder, increasing efficiency and productivity, thus helping the economy produce more output. Full employment may make workers slack off as they can’t be replaced hence productivity may fall and the economy may experience a loss of output.

M11

Economic Growth

Supply-Side

Which country has the largest economy in the world?

USA - $19.3 trillion

Supply-Side

What ranking does the UK hold when comparing the size of national economies?

The UK is the 5 th largest economy in the world ($2.62 trillion) and the second largest in Europe behind Germany ($3.68 trillion).

Others in the list include China ($12 trillion), Japan ($4.9 trillion)

Supply-Side

What % of the economy does the service sector account for in the UK?

79%

Supply-Side

What % of the economy does manufacturing account for?

10% of the economy

Supply-Side

Define 'Short Run Economic Growth'

An increase in output (real GDP).

Supply-Side

Define 'Long Run Economic Growth'

An increase in the productive potential of the economy.

Supply-Side

When does Long run growth only occur?

An increase in the quality or quantity of factors of production.

This can be shown by: A shift of the PPF curve or a shift of the LRAS curve

Supply-Side

Why does long run growth matter to an economy?

Without long run growth, short run growth will eventually halt as a supply constraint will be reached, where no more can be produced.

Supply-Side

Define 'GDP' - Gross Domestic Product

Gross Domestic Product = Total output produced by the factors of production.

Supply-Side

Define 'Nominal GDP'

Total output measured in current prices, and so not adjusted for inflation.

One of the problems of using nominal GDP to measure economic growth is that prices have increased over time, meaning an increase in GDP may simply signify an increase in the price level rather than in the size of the economy. Nominal GDP is thus often distorted by inflation, meaning we need a more accurate method of measuring an increase in output - Real GDP

Supply-Side

Define 'Real GDP'

GDP adjusted for inflation, sometimes known as GDP at constant prices.

Supply-Side

Define 'GDP Per Capita'

Output per head of the population. This is calculated by dividing GDP by the total population.

Supply-Side

What are the 3 methods to measure GDP?

The expenditure method: Is the sum total of all expenditures made by individuals on personal consumption, private investment and government purchases. It is measured through C+I+G+(X-M). In the above diagram, this is shown by the payment to firms.

The income method: This is all the total sum of factor payments received during a time period. This includes rent, profit, wages/salaries, interest and dividends. In the above diagram this is shown by the income flowing to the factors of production.

The output method: This is the value of all goods and services produced in the economy. In this method it is the value of materials added by producers at each stage of production that is considered. In the above diagram this is shown by the value of goods and services flowing to households.

Value added = Value of output – Cost of intermediate goods.

All three methods should provide the same answer.

Supply-Side

What are the difficulties of measuring Economic Growth?

A major problem with using GDP as a measure of economic growth is that it is highly likely that it will underestimate total output in the economy.This is because there is some output which will inevitably go unrecorded in GDP figures because of:

Moreover, even if it was possible to measure output accurately, it is not clear that economic growth defined in this way necessarily improves standard of living – which is after all the point of aiming to increase output in the first place!

Supply-Side

What indicators measure the quality of life?

(Helps measure the standard of living)

The key point is that if economic growth has been achieved by sacrificing one of the other quality of life indicators, or if growth has been achieved by people sacrificing leisure time, then people may not actually be better off as a result of economic growth.

Supply-Side

Why do governments want to achieve economic growth?

Increasing material living standards, reducing poverty

Supply-Side

Define 'Sustainable Economic Growth'

Growth which does not harm future generations’ ability to expand the productive capacity of the economy.

Governments particularly want steady and sustainable growth which avoids harmful fluctuations in economic activity.

Supply-Side

Why is sustainability considered an increasingly important target?

Because if you achieve growth by damaging resources then all you are doing is enjoying growth at the expense of a future generation.

Supply-Side

How can sustainable economic growth be achieved?

Growth has to occur without depleting non-renewable resources and damaging the environment. Measures seeking to reduce pollution and utilise cleaner sources of energy represents a step towards sustainable growth.

M12

Economic Growth - Causes and Consequences

Supply-Side

What can cause economic growth?

Economic growth can occur as a result of an increase in:

In the short run, an economy with spare capacity can experience economic growth as a result of increased aggregate demand

Supply-Side

Illustrate Economic Growth on AD/AS Diagram

Supply-Side

What are the benefits of economic growth?

FactorExplanation
Rise in material living standardsIf GDP rises faster than the growth in the population – ie GDP per capita rises – everyone on average has more output available to consume. People become richer as a consequence. They can take more holidays abroad, eat out at pubs and buy flat screen televisions. This improves our quality of life.
Rise in the welfare of the populationAs the capacity of the economy to produce more grows, the government can devote more resources to health and education. This is because they are getting more income from taxing the higher earnings. This benefits the welfare of individuals, particularly in developing countries where growth enables citizens to enjoy health and education they were previously lacking. Can also allow for improved literacy rates and reduced infant mortality.
Reduced unemployment and increased tax revenueLabour is a form of derived demand. As the economy grows and incomes increase, people are able to afford more goods and services increasing the demand for workers. The government also gets more revenue from taxes (both direct and indirect taxes)
Reduction in povertyAs output and incomes rise, the government can take more in taxes from the higher income groups and will be able to use it to improve the quality of life of the poor, of whom there are fewer because some of them are now in work due to a reduced unemployment rate.
Increased power on the international stageCountries with higher output have more power, so can negotiate trade agreements which favour them. In recent years, China has taken an increasingly important role on the world stage.
Accelerator effectThe accelerator effect happens when an increase in national income results in a proportionately larger rise in capital investment. It leads to more consumer goods and services improving the quality of life. For example, when an economy starts to recover from a recession, confidence is given a boost by the extra consumer spending. If they feel this spending will be sustained, firms are more likely to expand production by investing in capital equipment.
Supply-Side

What are the costs of economic growth?

FactorExplanation
Environmental costsGreater output can lead to more pollution of the land, air, sea and fresh water, as well as noise pollution. Demand for normal goods will usually increase leading to more road congestion increasing pollution. For example, rising income has increased demand for cars, air travel which adds to environmental damage.
Lower quality of lifeWhilst people may be better off materially, it can change people’s lifestyles, sometimes to their disadvantage – eg they may work longer hours without an improvement in productivity. Ultimately the rate at which an economy grows at depends on the output per worker. Growth that relies on population growing is unsustainable. Increased GDP growth doesn’t necessarily mean greater productivity.
Inequalities of wealth and incomeGenerally there is a positive relationship between economic growth and inequality. Very high growth rates are associated with income and wealth being concentrated in the hands of a few people. Countries such as China and India have high inequality.
InflationSometimes the rate of economic growth is too fast, when AD increases at a quicker rate than AS, for the economy to respond (in terms of increasing the number of resources), this can cause inflationary pressure. When it is because AD is growing too quickly, this is known as: demand pull inflation and this is not sustainable.
Supply-Side

Evaluation of the consequences of Economic Growth

Whether or not growth is beneficial to an economy depends upon:

• What type of growth is taking place.

We can illustrate this on a PPF diagram:

People may suffer in the short term because they have less goods and services to enjoy.

• Whether the growth comes from technology driving productivity:

• Whether growth is balanced:

• Whether everyone benefits.

• Whether growth creates inflationary pressure:

• Whether growth leads to a deficit on the balance of payments:

• Whether the savings ratio in the economy is falling:

Supply-Side

Define 'Recession'

A recession is two quarters or more of negative economic growth.

Supply-Side

What impact does a recession have on citizens of a country?

When there is negative economic growth, output has fallen, so less workers are required to produce a lower amount of output by firms so the derived demand for labour decreases and unemployment rises. Consumers now have less real disposable income hence their purchasing power for goods and services decreases leading to a fall in their material standards of living and quality of life.

Supply-Side

What causes recessions?

Some recessions are caused by falling AD. This was evident in 2008 with the global recessions. Banks became reluctant to lend to consumers and businesses reducing both consumption and investment. This would have led to lower profits for businesses creating further job losses and even deeper cuts in consumption.

Recessions can also be caused by ‘supply-side shocks’. If for example the price of oil increases, this will increase costs of production for firms leading to a fall in supply of goods and services. At the same time increased costs of production will be passed on to consumers and firms will lay off workers creating unemployment. This type of recession is also knows as ‘stagflation’.

Supply-Side

Features of a recession

Fall in real GDP, rising unemployment, falling business/consumer confidence, fall in spending and investment, falling tax revenue and increased government spending.

Supply-Side

How does a recession impact the government?

When there is negative economic growth, unemployment increases. The government has to give out more unemployment benefits such as JSA. Since less people are working the government also collects less tax revenue with less income tax received and less NI contributions. Consumers have less real disposable income so spend less revenue from VAT also falls. Consumers demand less goods and services from firms who see a fall in their profits and subsequently pay less corporation tax, hence the government collects less income tax revenue.

Supply-Side

How does a recession affect other economic agents?

(3)

M13

Multiplier

Analysis

Define 'Multiplier Effect'

The process by which any change in a component of aggregate demand results in a greater final change in real GDP.

Analysis

Define 'Negative Effect'

When an initial fall in spending causes a greater final fall in real GDP.

Analysis

Define 'Marginal Propensity to Withdraw'

MPW is the proportion of the change in income that leaks out of the circular flow of income.

The MPW = Marginal Propensity to Save (MPS) + Marginal Propensity to Tax (MPT)

The multiplier will have a big impact on the economy provided the Marginal Propensity to Withdraw is low.

Analysis

What are the conditions as to which the multiplier effect will be most effective?

A further factor which determines whether the initial round of spending will create a large knock-on effect on other components is the amount of:

FactorExplanation
Propensity to saveIf the propensity to save is low the multiplier will have a significant impact on the economy
Propensity to taxIf the marginal rate of tax on extra income is low the multiplier will have a significant impact on the economy
Propensity to importIf the marginal propensity to import is low the multiplier will have a significant impact on the economy
Analysis

How do you calculate the multiplier?

The formula for the multiplier is: 1/1-mpc (mpc = marginal propensity to consume) OR 1/mpw (mpw = marginal propensity to withdraw)

Analysis

Define 'Austerity'

Austerity refers to strict economic policies that a government imposes to control growing public debt.

Analysis

Illustrate the 'Economic Cycle'

The dotted line represents:

The solid line represents:

Analysis

Define 'Actual Rate of GDP Growth'

Short term changes in the level of output measured by changes in real GDP.

Analysis

Define 'Trend Rate of Growth'

Long term changes in the productive capacity of the economy.

Analysis

Define 'Positive Output Gap'

When actual rate > trend rate.

Analysis

Illustrate the 'Positive Output Gap'

Analysis

Define 'Negative Output Gap'

When actual rate < trend rate.

Analysis

Illustrate the 'Negative Output Gap'

Analysis

Can an economy run a positive output gap?

In the short run an economy can produce more than its productive capacity. Some people will be tempted to enter the labour force because of the higher wages. Others will work overtime.

Analysis

What are the consequences of both outputs?

Negative Output Gaps: - Unemployment - Possible deflation

Positive Output Gaps: - Inflation - Possible Wage price Spiral

M14

Trends in Macroeconomic Indicators

See module notes for this section.

M15

Fiscal Policy

Policy

Define 'Fiscal Policy'

Fiscal policy is the use of taxation and government spending to manipulate aggregate demand

Policy

What are the types of tax?

TaxExplanation
Income taxThe most significant tax in the UK. Direct and progressive - as a person’s income rises, the rate of tax they pay increases. Direct tax - means tax on income Indirect tax - tax on spending Progressive - as a person's income rises, the rate of tax they increase. less than £12,500 = 0% tax £12,501 - £37,500 = 20% tax £37,501 - £150,000 = 40% £150,001 = 45% Regressive tax - poorer you are the more tax you pay P1 - £100 weekly income - buys a tv for £100 so will have to pay 20%/20£ VAT - that’s 20% of their income P2 - £10,000 weekly income - buys tv for £100, still 20% VAT, that is 0.2% of their income
VATIndirect and largely regressive - standard rate of 20% (increased from 17.5% recently) paid regardless of income - imposed on the sale of most goods and services. Some items are taxed at 5% and some, such as most food, children's clothes and books, do not have VAT charged in them as they are seen as necessities.
Excise dutyIndirect tax imposed on specific products, such as alcohol and tobacco. The rate varies depending on the product. Heavily taxed.
Capital gains taxTax on the increase in the value of investments, such a shares and second homes. This is a tax on wealth.
Corporation taxA tax on the profits of firms, currently 19%.
Inheritance taxTaxes the wealth of the deceased above a certain level of wealth.
Policy

What are the types of Government Spending?

Type of SpendingExplanation
Capital expenditureOn hospitals, schools, roads etc. This spending increases the productive potential of the economy. Cuts to this type of spending is more detrimental than current spending.
Current spendingOn the running of public services including teachers pay and the purchase of medicines to be used in the NHS.
Transfer paymentsMoney transferred from taxpayers to recipients of benefits.
Debt interest paymentsPayment made to to holders of government debt (bonds)
Policy

How is the Government Budget Outlined?*

Government spending proposals are outlined in the budget. The Chancellor of the Exchequer also announces any tax changes and provides information on the budget position in the past year and in the years to come.

The budget position shows the relationship between government spending and tax revenue, with a balanced budget occurring when the two are equal.

In reality, budget deficits and budget surpluses are more likely occurrences. In recessions governments tend to have large budget deficits, causing a build up of government debt. This is the position the UK economy finds itself in today.

DO NOT CONFUSE BUDGET DEFICIT WITH BUDGET PAYMENT DEFICIT AND THE SAME FOR SURPLUS.

Budget payment deficit is when a country exports less than their imports. exports

Policy

Define 'Expansionary Fiscal Policy' and why is it useful?

Expansionary fiscal policy is rises in government spending and cuts in taxes designed to increase AD.

o When might the government want to use expansionary fiscal policy? In times of economic downturn to stimulate the economy, achieving economic growth and reducing unemployment.

Running a Budget Deficit is expansionary and shifts AD to the right

Policy

Illustrate the concept of 'Expansionary Fiscal Policy'

Policy

Define 'Contractionary Fiscal Policy' and why is it useful?

Contractionary fiscal policy is designed to reduce the level of aggregate demand in an economy through cuts in spending or increases in taxation.

o When might the government want to use contractionary fiscal policy? When the economy is over-heating to reduce inflation.

Running a budget surplus is contractionary and shifts AD to the left.

Policy

Illustrate the concept of 'Contractionary Fiscal Policy'

Policy

Define 'Discretionary Fiscal Policy'

When governments actively influence AD by changing expenditure or taxes.

Policy

What are the 'Fiscal Rules' and why do we have them?

Clearly, there is an incentive for the government to enact expansionary fiscal policy in the build up to a general election and contractionary fiscal policy immediately after it. This manipulation of fiscal policy to achieve political objectives can have damaging effects on the economy, creating an environment of uncertainty and instability. In an attempt to avoid this, the New Labour government created ‘fiscal rules’ – a method of self-governance designed to improve the credibility of fiscal policy. The rules were:

Policy

Why are the 'Fiscal Rules' been rendered redundant in the recent years?

They were flawed to start with – definition of what the ‘economic cycle’ is was stretched.

But more importantly, financial crisis meant the government had to borrow a significant amount of money. Debt/GDP ratio at present stands at around 85% of GDP for the UK.

Policy

What are the advantages of Fiscal Policy?

FactorExplanation
Policy works automatically to stabilise the economy.A number of forms of government spending adjust automatically to offset fluctuations in GDP – fiscal policy can therefore stabilise the economy without any real action required.
Changes in government spending directly affect aggregate demand.They don’t rely on a complicated transmission mechanism to have a final effect on the economy. For example increasing spending in the economy will put money into people’s pockets directly. With monetary policy there is a time lag and even then the cuts in interest rates may not be passed on to consumers.
Have the potential to influence both AD and AS simultaneously.Eg. cutting corporation tax, which means impact on macroeconomy is significant. Government can decide when to spend on infrastructure which again has a more direct impact. You’re not relying on private sector to provide growth which never comes (confidence in recession). With monetary policy this may not happen due to low confidence in the economy.
Policy

What are the disadvantages of Fiscal Policy?

FactorExplanation
Time lags exist.It takes time to form and implement new policy (such as changing the tax rate). It also might take time for households to adjust to a change in their disposable income, meaning spending might not immediately increase when the tax rate is cut.
Some forms of government spending are inflexible.Once you decide to build a new school, it’s difficult to withdraw from that investment or make adjustments without creating a political storm.
Difficult to estimate the size of the multiplier and MPCThe policy might have a greater or smaller impact on the economy than desired. Success is very much determined by the marginal propensity to consume (MPC). If it is small, then the investment may leak out of the circular flow.
May have an adverse effect on incentives.A rise in income tax may discourage some people from working and higher corporation tax might discourage investment.
May damage other macroeconomic objectives.A rise in taxation designed to reduce inflation may increase unemployment.
May be offset by circumstances in other countries (ceteris paribus may not hold)If the UK is pursuing expansionary fiscal policy but the US experiences recession AD may still fall if the economy is reliant on exports.
Policy

Define 'The Government Budget'

The amount of money that the government has, to spend, financed by tax revenue.

Policy

Define 'Budget Deficit'

If tax receipts are less than the amount of money the government spends then the government’s budget is in deficit.

Policy

Define 'Budget Surplus'

If tax receipts are greater than the amount of money the government spends then the government’s budget is in surplus.

Policy

Define 'Government Bonds'

A bond issued by a country's government, promising to repay borrowed money a fixed rate of interest at a specified time.

This helps finance Budget Deficit.

Policy

Define 'Structural Budget Deficit'

The issue of spending more than governments receive - when governments run deficits over a long period of time.

Such deficits aren’t a concern to economists.

Policy

Define 'Cyclical Deficit'

Deficits which are short-term and caused by automatic stabilisers

Such deficits aren’t a concern to economists.

Policy

Define 'National Debt'

The accumulated borrowings of government.

Policy

What is the difference between 'National Debt' and 'Budget Deficit'

The national debt is the total amount of money the government owes. The budget deficit is the difference between tax revenue and government expenditure in a specific year. Long term budgets deficits add up to cumulatively create the total national debt.

Policy

Illustrate the 3 types of tax

Policy

Define 'Direct Tax'

A tax on income or wealth.

Policy

Define 'Indirect Tax'

A tax levied on goods and services.

Policy

What are the advantages and the disadvantages of 'Progressive Tax'?

AdvantagesDisadvantages
It takes more taxes from those who can afford to pay them. High income earners can afford to meet their basic needs and have money left over that would have been saved which can be used for the redistribution of income and payment for public services such as the NHS.It disincentives work, high income earners will lose much of their salary (45% over £100,000 in the UK), this may stop people opening businesses, going for promotions, and may even cause people to go and live abroad where tax is lower. This will have an adverse effect on the quantity and quality of labour and may restrict AS.
It allows very low wage earners to be charged no tax at all (upto £11,000 in the UK pay no tax at all). This prevents poverty and it also incentivises work as it means benefits are significantly better than benefits. If low salaries were taxed it may mean people were not much better of working than claiming benefits and they may choose not to work. We call this ‘the poverty trap’.It may also disincentives people from paying their taxes, people may try and avoid tax which is using complex accounting structures to pay the least tax possible. The high rate of tax may actually reduce the amount of tax revenue that the government collects.
UntitledSome people think it is unfair and punishes success.
Policy

What are the advantage and the disadvantages of 'Regressive Tax'?

AdvantagesDisadvantages
It is easy to administrate. The same tax is charged to all per item. E.g. tax on a box of 20 cigarettes is around £8 whether you earn £80 a day or £800 a day. However it is a much bigger percentage of income if you are earning £80.It has a regressive effect: Takes a much higher percentage of income from low income earners and as such punishes poor people more for engaging in the same activity as high income earners. It restricts their quality of life and may cause poverty.
It targets demerit goods which are more likely to be used by low income earners such as alcohol, gambling, tobacco etc. This may make them more likely to stop them using these goods.Such goods may be price inelastic in their demand as they are habit forming. This means despite the tax people may not be able to stop using them. As a result they may have to make savings in other areas such as children’s food, heating or clothes in order to pay the regressive tax.
Can help to reduce the market failure arising from goods such as petrol, cigarette and alcohol, ‘internalising the externality’ which has been caused. (use this phrase as they will be comfortable with it from micro)Low income houses tend to have a higher MPC than higher income households who may save, this may expose them even more so to the regressive nature of VAT.
Policy

Define 'Flat-rate tax system' - not part of the specification (Italian System Suggestion)

A system of income tax in which each taxpayer pays the same rate of tax regardless of income.

Policy

What are the advantages and the disadvantages of a 'Flat-rate tax system'?

It does not allow for the redistribution of income in the same way as a progressive tax system; which takes the most from the high income earners that can afford it and the least from the low income earners that can’t. Income can then be redistributed to low income earners in the form of benefits and tax credits. It also does not allow lower/no taxes to be charged to very low income earners which may mean they fall into the ‘poverty trap’; whereby the taxes on their wages mean the money they get to take home is not much higher than their benefits, as such they may choose not to work increasing the benefits bill and harming the supply side of the economy as the quantity of labour falls.

Most income from taxes is contributed by the top 10% (more than half in the UK). A flat tax would damage a countries ability to pay for essential public services.

M16

Monetary Policy

Demand-Side

Why is interest rates etc. controlled by an independent body and not the government?

The MPC consists of nine members – the Governor and two deputy Governors of the Bank of England, and four economists appointed by the Chancellor. As it is very difficult to target inflation precisely, a 1% margin is set either side of the target, meaning inflation in the range of 1-3% is considered acceptable. If inflation is outside of this target range, the Governor of the Bank of England has to write a letter to the Chancellor explaining why the Bank has failed to meet the inflation target. Inflation is the primary target of the MPC, but they are also mindful of the government’s other macroeconomic objectives.

What evidence is there that the MPC has in recent years actively considered objectives other than inflation when setting interest rates?

It is also important to know that the MPC is always forward thinking – they make changes to the rate of interest now intending to control the inflation rate in two years time. Why does the MPC look this far ahead when setting interest rates?

Demand-Side

Define 'Monetary Policy'

Use of interest rate, the money supply and exchange rates designed to manipulate the level of aggregate demand in an economy.

Demand-Side

What is the 'Monetary Policy Transmission Mechanism'?

Monetary policy is primarily enacted to achieve the government’s target of 2% inflation. The main method used to do this is: controlling the interest rate. If inflation is too high, to control this, the decision is taken to: increase the rate of interest. The transmission mechanism outlines the steps from the policy decision to achieving the desired reduction in inflation:

In addition to the impact on consumption and investment we have analysed above, an increase in the interest rate can also have an impact on the balance of payments. This is because, when interest rates rise, foreigners are attracted to save and invest in the country. This increases the demand for pounds, which causes an appreciation in the exchange rate. This causes international competitiveness to worsen because: exports now become more expensive and imports cheaper. This causes aggregate demand to fall because: (X-M), a component of AD decreases.

Demand-Side

Illustrate 'Expansionary Monetary Policy'

Demand-Side

Illustrate 'Contractionary Monetary Policy'

Demand-Side

What is the alternative form of Monetary Policy?

It is also possible to enact monetary policy through changing the money supply . The effects on the economy are identical to those achieved through changing interest rates, with an extra stage added at the start of the transmission mechanism.

A decrease in the money supply will cause the interest rate to increase .

This is because: banks have less money to lend to customers.

The result of this is that aggregate demand falls, for all the reasons analysed previously, leading to the same effects on the macroeconomy as if interest rates had been increased

Demand-Side

The ways they implement this:

They do this by adding to ‘Central Banks reserves’ which commercial banks hold with the Bank of England.

OR

Purchasing assets from the government or commercial banks. This replaces debts or bonds with actual liquid capital adding to central banks reserves and means banks are more willing to lend as the money supply has now increased.

Demand-Side

Why may they have wanted to do this?

QE can work in a couple of ways. The obvious and easy way is to add to ‘reserves’.

Demand-Side

Why using QE very limited/not work as expected?

Low levels of confidence

Demand-Side

Who sets the level of Interest Rates and with what in mind?

Since May 1997, interest rates have been set by the Monetary Policy Committee (MPC). This operates independently from the government and is tasked with setting the interest rate to achieve the government’s target of an annual rate of inflation of 2%.

They are also mindful of the other macroeconomic objectives.

Demand-Side

Exercise 1:

Using the chart explain why despite very low interest rates, inflation fluctuated between 2009 and 2017?

M17

Supply-Side Policy

Supply-Side

Define 'Supply-side Policies'

Policies designed to increase the level of aggregate supply in the economy and make markets work more efficiently.

Supply-Side

What is the difference between 'Supply-Side Policies' and ' Supply-Side Reforms'?

Supply side policies can be implemented by the government (interventionist) or they can be reforms undertaken by the private sector (market based). It is important to make this distinction between supply-side policies and supply-side reforms or improvements. These ‘reforms’ are undertaken by private firms as they must maintain efficiency and remain competitive to survive in a globalised world. This contrasts with supply-side policies undertaken by the government, whose actions to improve the supply-side of the economy are a means to enabling the desirable outcome mentioned above.

Supply-Side

Illustrate the impact of supply side policies on the economy using the Keynesian and Classical AS curves

Note: AS in the Keynesian curve can also be LRAS (Long Run Aggregate Supply)

Supply-Side

Examples of Supply-Side Policies

PolicyExplanation
Education and training(Interventionist SSP)Can increase the quality of factors of production by making workers more productive and improving human capital. It makes them more employable, increasing employment in areas which have skills shortages.
Government assistance to new firms(Interventionist SSP)Government could give tax breaks and grants to newly formed companies to encourage innovation. This could increase the amount of entrepreneurship.
Research and development grants(Interventionist SSP)Will encourage the development of capital and new methods of innovation process reducing costs in the long term.
Reduction in direct taxes(Market based SSP)Cut in corporation tax could incentivise firms to invest, increasing the quantity of capital. Whether cutting income tax will increase or decrease the quantity of labour is debatable – may encourage people into the labour market or to work longer hours.
National minimum wage(Market base SSP)Might incentivise people into the labour market, but also might create inefficiencies.
Reduction in benefits(Market based SSP)Cutting benefits may encourage the economically inactive into the labour market.
Reduction in trade union power(Market based SSP)May increase labour productivity by reducing labour market inefficiencies. It allows businesses to improve productivity by making them work harder and employing more workers as the pressure to increase wages isn’t as intense. Telecoomunications, gas, electricity and BA were all privatised in the 80’s.
Privatisation(Market based SSP)Private sector firms can more efficiently decide what to produce and how to produce it as they are motivated by efficiency (reducing costs) and increasing profit.
Deregulation(Market based SSP)Cutting red tape can increase efficiency. Also can allow new firms to enter the industry more easily. Examples of de-regulation: De-regulation of banking has created jobs in the city of London, The ease with which entrepreneurs can start businesses in the UK, The rise of flexible labour markets (note: this in it self could be another factor)
Increasing competition(Market based SSP)Leads to improved efficiencies, better products, lower prices for consumers.
Improving Infrastructure(Interventionist SSP)Improving the quality of roads, airports, ports, broadband connections, water/gas/electricity networks will reduce costs for business and increase the amount that the economy is capable of producing. This will boost the level of investment by private firms in the economy as investment becomes more profitable as they do not have to meet costs of infrastructure themselves.
Supply-Side

Define 'Productivity'

Output per worker over a given time period.

Supply-Side

Why is there low overall productivity in the UK?

The lack of investment in infrastructure and education.

Other cited reasons include:

Supply-Side

What 'Supply-Side Policies' help improve productivity?

Supply side policies such as investment in education, training and infrastructure could help improve productivity.

In theory market based approaches such as privatisation and de-regulation should also help.

This is because, the private sector:

Supply-Side

What is the aim of the policy?

Prevent negative output gaps and unemployment and create growth.

Supply-Side

What is the conflict with this policy?

Demand-pull inflation if there is limited capacity caused by shortage of resources and possible wage-price spiral.

Supply-Side

Illustrate the conflict on an AD/AS diagram

Supply-Side

What is the aim of the policy?

Prevent inflation.

Supply-Side

What is the conflict with this policy?

It can create negative output gaps and reduce real GDP and economic growth.

Supply-Side

Illustrate the conflict on an AD/AS diagram

Supply-Side

What is the aim of the policy?

Designed to prevent negative output gaps and unemployment and create growth.

Supply-Side

What is the conflict with this policy?

Demand-pull inflation if there is limited capacity caused by shortage of resources and possible wage-price spiral. Inflation might also damage international competitiveness of UK produced goods reducing exports.

There is also evidence to suggest that economic growth exacerbates inequality. This is because

the benefits of growth go disproportionately to the wealthy and owners of capital.

Supply-Side

Illustrate the conflict on an AD/AS diagram

Supply-Side

What is the aim of the Policy?

Designed to prevent inflation and growth (in the UK)

Supply-Side

What is the conflict with this policy?

It can create negative output gaps and reduce the value of real GDP and economic growth

Supply-Side

Illustrate the conflict on an AD/AS diagram

Supply-Side

How can these policy conflicts be avoided?

Growth and inflation is achievable at the same time if the governments invests in supply-side policies as it creates spare capacity in the economy.

Policies such as privatisation and deregulation can create competition in the economy driving efficiency and incentivising firms to lower prices. At the same time these firms might innovate allowing consumers to benefit from greater range of goods and services as well as higher living standards.

Supply-Side

What policies could help in overcoming cyclical unemployment?

Fiscal and/or monetary policy will shift out AD and be effective.

Government spending will be more effective than tax cuts because government spending will directly affect AD, whereas there might be leakages (in the form of saving) when tax cuts are introduced.

Tax cuts are also difficult to justify if they are cut for the top earners only.

Generally, fiscal policy has been used to achieve economic stability and monetary policy has been used to control inflation. But whilst neither have been used to target unemployment directly, it is certainly true that both of the objectives will help indirectly achieve stable employment.

Supply-Side

Which policy would be most effective in overcoming structural and frictional unemployment?

Supply side policies would be most effective, such as:

Improve information about jobs.

Offer more training opportunities.

Greater provision of low cost childcare.

Increase the economic incentive to work – raise minimum wage or lower benefits.

Relax rules on hiring and firing.

Supply-Side

Which policy would be most effective in controlling cost-push inflation?

Government would need to lower firms’ costs, using supply side policy. Either cut the rate of corporation tax or increase subsidies.

Difficult to control if caused by rising food and commodity prices as they are international in nature. The UK is too small to influence prices on the international markets. For example, prices may be rising due to increased demand in China or India.

Similarly if prices are rising because of a weak £ then there is very little the government can do as confidence in the UK economy may be the underlying problem.

Supply-Side

Which policy would be most effective in controlling demand pull inflation?

Most effective to use deflationary fiscal or monetary policy.

The primary policy used in the UK is changing the interest rate.

Supply-Side

In the long run, which policy ensures inflation stays low?
