Supply side, as economy doesn’t run out of capacity – ensure productive capacity is growing at least as quickly as AD.

(4)

M19

The Balance Of Payments

International

What is the Current Account?

The current account is a measure of the flow of funds from trade in goods and services, plus other income flows.

International

What are the 4 components of the current account?

ComponentExplanation
The Balance of Trade in GoodsOften called visible trade, the balance of trade in goods represents actual tangible products.
The Balance of Trade in servicesOften called invisible trade, the balance of trade in services represents intangible items. Examples include banking and finance, insurance, telecommunications, transport and tourism
Primary incomeThis is movements investment income which consists of profits, interest, dividends and migrant remittances (payment from people living and working overseas).
Secondary incomeThis is also known as current transfers which is a record of the exchange of money for which no goods or services change hands. Money spent on foreign aid and to the European Union is an example. Other examples include donations to charities and overseas Aid.
International

Differ between a Financial account surplus and deficit (similar question)

When the flow of money into the UK economy is greater than the flow of money leaving the country, the UK will run a:

When the flow of money out of the UK economy is greater than the flow of money in to the country, the UK will run a:

International

Is the current account in surplus or in deficit?

The deficit caused by the trade in goods is too big. In recent years the deficit has been made worse by the deficit on the primary income.

International

What is the Financial Account?

The financial account measures the net change in foreign ownership of domestic financial assets.

International

What are the three components of the Financial account?

Direct Investment (Direct Capital Flows)

This is investment in capital assets by a company with its headquarters in a foreign country. It is also known as Foreign Direct Investment (FDI).

How attractive is the UK in terms of FDI?

Portfolio Investment (Portfolio Capital Flows)

This the purchase of a country’s securities such as bonds, shares and derivatives by the residents or financial institutions of another country.

Reserve Assets

This section records the official reserves of foreign currencies and gold held by central banks.

Any money going out of the financial account is recorded as a ‘debit’ and money coming in is recorded as a ‘credit’.

International

What is the Capital Account?

Trade in assets - an insignificant part of the balance so has little to no effect.

International

How could we balance the balance of payments?

When we hear of imbalances on the balance of payment, we are referring to a current account deficit or surplus.

A deficit on one of the accounts much be matched by a surplus in the other. Thus, when we say that the UK runs a deficit on the current account, it must mean that it also runs a surplus on the financial account.

The reason for the above is this:

This must mean:

OR

M20

Imbalances in The Balance Of Payments

International

Define Current Account

The balance of trade in goods and services plus net investment incomes from overseas assets.

International

Define Current Account Surplus

The value of exports exceeds the value of imports. There is an inflow of money.

International

Define Current Account Deficit

The value of imports exceeds the value of exports. There is an outflow of money.

International

Why does the UK run a Current Account Deficit?

FactorExplanation
Loss of advantage in many industriesUK used to have an absolute advantage in industries such as iron and steel, shipbuilding and textiles. This absolute advantage has gone and these goods are now largely imported. This has resulted in global interdependence, UK firms are sophisticated and have global supply chains. Domestic producers buy their raw materials and semi-finished goods from the most efficient and cost-effective foreign producers with an absolute advantage. Imports are critical to producers in the UK because the UK has deindustrialised and reallocated many resources to specialising in the production of tertiary products, resulting in a reliance on foreign producers irrespective of how the UK economy is performing.
GlobalisationCheaper to produce goods where labour costs are low, meaning many goods which were made in the UK are now made in India and China as firms have moved production and other operations abroad. Some MNCs operating in the UK have relocated to foreign countries and exported their technology away. This has diminished UK’s productive capacity in key primary and secondary sectors, leading to a reliance on a vast range of goods from abroad (global interdependence). This generates a goods deficit which outweighs the services trade surplus.
Growth in real incomesDemand has risen more quickly than supply, meaning people have looked abroad to imports. As income rises so does the demand for imports suggesting that the YED for imports is positive. As a result the expenditure on imports is greater than the revenue received from exports.
Strong exchange rate and exchange rate appreciationThe strength of the £ throughout the 90’s and most of the 00’s meant that imports were cheaper and exports expensive. Demand for imports was therefore high worsening the deficit on the current account of the balance of payment.
Low levels of productivity and investmentLabour productivity is low in the UK, largely due to poor levels of capital investment, and low levels of technical education. This increases the cost per unit for UK exports, therefore increasing the price, reducing overall demand. This also makes imports cheaper increasing demand for them.
Relatively weak product innovationLow rate of spending on research and development compared to other advanced economies has led to lack of innovation. Domestic consumers have increasingly switched to imports, resulting in import expenditure levels exceed export revenue. E.G. How may consumer electronics are made in Britain?
International

What are the consequences of running a Current Account Deficit?

Causes AD to shift inwards which results in falling output, rising unemployment and downward pressure on the price level (Ceteris Paribus)

The oversupply of currency could also place downward pressure on the currency and create cost push inflationary pressure in the longer term.

International

What does the damage caused by running the Current Account Deficit to the economy depend on?

FactorExplanation
The length/time of the deficitA deficit caused by external factors such as a recession in a trading partner country may be insignificant but a deficit which is sustained is a problem because it may mean that the country is living beyond its means with very little savings. It also suggests that the deficit is being funded by excessive borrowing. If foreign institutions are buying up UK treasury gilts for example, we also have to pay interest on the borrowing, racking up the national debt.
Is the deficit funded through high levels of borrowing?A deficit on the current account is a problem if it is being funded by excessive borrowing. If foreign institutions are purchasing government securities, this translates into higher national debt owed to foreign institutions. If however, the deficit is financed through FDI then it will create jobs and growth.
Is the deficit caused by a loss of competitiveness?If the deficit is caused by a loss of competitiveness it suggests that the economy may be too focused on consumption like the UK and not enough focus is on investment (which is an indicator of competitiveness).
International

What are the problems with running a Current Account Deficit?

Risk of capital flight:

Foreign ownership of assets:

International

What proportion of the UKs' growth is Consumption?

65%

International

What are examples of these policies?

Successive governments have tried to solve the problem without much success. As the issue seems to be one of quality and cost the government is better off promoting supply-side policies to influence the level of demand for its exports.

PolicyExplanationEvaluation
Demand-side policies(Expenditure Reducing)This works by raising taxes or interest rates thus reducing disposable income of consumers. As the YED for imports is positive in the UK, a fall in income would also reduce the demand for imports. If the marginal propensity to import for the UK economy is 0.4 for example, a £1 billion fall in AD would lead to a fall in the expenditure of imports worth £400,000. This policy also has an element of expenditure switching element. As AD falls, the economy can experience deflation leading to a fall in domestic prices compared to imports so consumers switch to domestic goods and services.Harms the objective of economic growth and full employment. Depends on factors: • The size of the marginal propensity to import • Whether the economy is operating at full capacity. If it is operating under capacity, it is unwise to use this policy as it will make the recession worse.
Reduce the exchange rate by cutting interest rates(Expenditure Switching)Depreciation of the £ would lead to hot money flowing out, exchange rate depreciating and British goods becoming more price competitive. Cheaper domestic goods will increase demand for them. (Show through a shift of the supply curve to the right)Bad for consumers who will experience higher prices of imports affecting living standards of the poorest in the country. Increases the price of raw materials and therefore costs of production. As the UK imports significant amounts of raw material, this policy is counter-productive. Depends on factors: • The size and time length of the depreciation. Small movements in the currency will leave the deficit unaffected. • The price elasticity of demand (PED) of the product
Supply side policies (Expenditure Switching)Increasing investment in the economy to improve productivity will improve the price competitiveness of goods produced in the UK by reducing their cost per unit. (Show through a shift of the supply curve to the right) Measures such as improving the flexibility of the UK labour market would also help as it reduces cost for employers and so the savings may be passed on to consumers. Other measures include: Privatisation and de-regulation on a large scale to improve the efficiency of companies and therefore long term reductions in prices.Time consuming Expensive It assumes that rest of the world isn’t buying UK produced goods because they are expensive. This policy doesn’t work if the issue is with quality
Protectionism (Expenditure Switching)Tariffs and other protectionist measures such as quotas could reduce the amount of imports into the country by increasing their price or limiting the amount allowed into the country.Could lead to conflict with the WTO, UK can’t impose protectionism on other EU countries. Other countries will retaliate and therefore affect living standards as well as the price of commodities if the tariff is on commodities. Distorts the market mechanism as UK companies might end up producing goods and services which they do not have comparative advantage in.
M21

Exchange Rates

Analysis

Define Exchange Rate

The price of one currency in terms of another.

Analysis

What does an increase in the value of the pound mean?

One‌ ‌pound‌ ‌buys‌ ‌more‌ ‌of‌ ‌a‌ ‌foreign‌ ‌currency,‌ ‌or‌ ‌more‌ ‌of‌ ‌a‌ ‌foreign‌ ‌currency‌ ‌is‌ ‌

needed‌ ‌to‌ ‌buy‌ ‌one‌ ‌pound.‌

Analysis

What does an decrease in the value of the pound mean?

One pound buys less of a foreign currency, or less of a foreign currency is required to buy pounds.

Analysis

How are Exchange Rates determined?

There are three types of exchange rate systems in operation in the world economy today:

Analysis

Define Floating Exchange Rate

Where the prices of two currencies are determined by market forces.

Analysis

Illustrate the appreciation of the pound on a diagram

Analysis

What four factors influence the Exchange Rate?

Factor affecting exchange rateExplanation
Demand for imports and exportsIf a country runs a current account surplus they are likely to see their currency appreciate overtime as money flows into the country. This increases the demand for the currency and brings about an appreciation. If a country runs a deficit on the current account they are likely to see their currency depreciate overtime as the domestic currency is sold to buy imports. This increases the supply of the currency and leads to a depreciation
Flows of hot moneyHigh interest rates are likely to lead to an appreciation of the currency as money flows into financial institutions and banks.
Speculation on the currencyThis usually happens under fixed exchange rate systems. If markets feel the currency is overvalued there will be downward pressure on the currency leading to a depreciation.
Inward investmentA country that attracts investors will see a large increase in the demand for its currencies.
Analysis

Answer

Economic indicatorExplanation
GrowthX become more expensive, demand for them falls, M becomes cheaper, demand for them increases. As net exports falls, AD shifts inwards. The negative multiplier may cause further shifts of the AD curve to the left
UnemploymentDerived demand for labour falls, increasing unemployment rates
Current accountNegative, the deficit will widen
InflationPositive, as AD shifts inwards demand for resources falls, reducing their prices improving purchasing power. As import prices fall, input prices will also fall reducing cost-push inflationary pressure in the economy.
Analysis

What factors influence the damage/benefits the Exchange Rate has on the economy?

FactorExplanation
The scale of exchange rate movements.The smaller the movement the smaller the impact on the economy.
The time period under consideration.Short term movements which are temporary are unlikely to harm the economy. A sustained fall or rise in the currency are likely to have a bigger impact on the economy. This is because it highlights underlying issues such as investor confidence in the economy.
The industry in question.Some industries are more exposed to currency fluctuations than others. Companies that are reliant on cheap raw materials are most likely to be hit hard when the Sterling depreciates. These are likely to be manufacturing firms.
The size of any multiplier or accelerator effectsIf the economy is operating with spare capacity and the size of the multiplier is significant, then the economy will benefit from a devaluation of the currency and the impact on price level is likely to insignificant, provided the economy is not dependent on imports.
Which currency the £ depreciates against.UK trades mostly with the European Union. Therefore, any movement of the £ upwards against the Euro will be bad news for UK manufacturers.
Analysis

Define Marshall-Lerner Condition

The Marshall-Lerner condition states that the policy of devaluing a currency will only be successful for a country if the sum of the elasticity for its imports and exports is greater than 1.

Analysis

Define Fixed Exchange Rate

Are currencies which are tied to a particular currency such as the dollar or other commodities such as gold.

Analysis

Define Managed Exchange Rate

This is a combination of fixed and floating exchange rate system. Examples include Exchange Rate Mechanism (ERM) which Britain was part of from 1990 to 1992. Under this system, currencies were kept inside an agreed limit (+/-) 2.25%. This was achieved by manipulating interest rates or Central Banks buying or selling currency. The managed exchange rate is also known as a ‘dirty float’ or ‘adjustable peg’

Analysis

How to countries maintain a Fixed Exchange Rate?

In order to maintain a peg, the central bank of a country will intervene whenever the value of a currency deviates from the agreed limit.

Analysis

What is needed to successfully run a Fixed Exchange Rate?

For fixed exchange rates to work the two countries involved must have similar interest rates and inflation. To understand this think about what would happen if inflation or interest rate was higher in one country and not the other:

Analysis

What are the advantages and the disadvantages of running a Fixed Exchange Rate?

Advantages:

Disadvantages:

Analysis

Define Purchasing Power Parity (PPP)

The hypothesis suggests that once converted to a single currency goods and services should cost the same in every country.

Analysis

How is PPP measured?

One of these measures is the Big Mac Index developed by the Economist.

The average price of a Big Mac in America is $5.28. Therefore, Big Mac’s in all other countries should cost the same according to PPP theory. If it costs more, then that currency is overvalued. Any less than $5.28 and the currency is under-valued.

The average price of a Big Mac in the Euro Area is $4.84. This suggests that the Euro is undervalued by 8.4% ($5.28-$4.84/$5.28X100).

Analysis

Why is PPP important?

Purchasing power parity is used in many situations. The most common method is to adjust for the price differences between countries. For example, China produced $10.98 trillion in goods and services in 2015. The U.S. produced $17.95 trillion. You cannot compare the two without taking into account the fact that the cost of living in China is much lower than in the United States. People in China don't need as much income because it costs less to live.

Purchasing power parity solves the problem of comparing countries with different standards of living. It recalculates the value of a country's goods and services as if they were being sold at U.S. prices. Under PPP, a Chinese Big Mac costs $5.28, the same as it does in the United States. As a result, China's GDP under PPP is $19.39 trillion. That makes it the world's largest economy, ahead of the European Union and the United States. That's why the CIA provides GDP estimates on both an official exchange rate and a purchasing power parity basis.

Without purchasing power parity, China's GDP per capita would only be around $6,475. That's lower than the standard of living in Ukraine, Algeria or Kosovo.

Analysis

How realistic is PPP?

It is however worth noting that PPP can be restored (Again in theory and provided Ceteris Paribus holds) if American’s change holiday destinations to the Eurozone (there will be an appreciation in the Euro)

Costs of production differ between countries i.e. rent and wages which are higher in developed countries. This could explain why the price of a Big Mac is dearer in a country like Switzerland and cheaper in Turkey.

Costs of items can also differ because of protectionism or other forms of taxes. What’s more, some things like haircuts can’t be exported.
